summary: Joe Reis argues that data teams make a fundamental mistake by focusing on tools before understanding business needs. He emphasizes that the order of operations matters: start by understanding what stakeholders want, then select appropriate tools—never the reverse. Tool discussions without business context are distracting and lead to building things nobody asked for.
key_points: Business case must come before tool selection
Tool-first approaches lead to building things nobody wants
Tooling discussions often devolve into unproductive trivia contests
Understanding stakeholder needs is the essential first step
money_quotes: No matter where I go in the world, the question of 'What tools should I use?' pops up repeatedly.
Data teams often choose the path that doesn't work, jumping straight to #2. This is a massive mistake; inevitably, many things are built that nobody asked for and don't add value.
Business case, then tools. Never the reverse.

Tooling discussions usually devolve into a pissing match of how much trivia everyone knows about particular tools. While those conversations are fun occasionally, they're highly distracting and usually a waste of time.
key_insight: The universal obsession with tooling in data teams is a distraction from the real work of understanding and solving business problems.
